critique.summary: The article introduces Zeroboot, a project that implements sub-millisecond virtual machine (VM) sandboxes using copy-on-write (CoW) memory forking. It highlights the performance benchmarks, architecture, and SDKs available for running isolated KVM VMs with hardware-enforced memory isolation, aimed at AI agents and code execution.
mainPoints: Zeroboot achieves VM sandbox spawn latencies as low as 0.79 milliseconds (p50) using CoW memory forking.
Each sandbox is a real KVM virtual machine with hardware-enforced memory isolation.
The system uses a Firecracker snapshot as a template, which is then forked with CoW memory mapping to create new VMs quickly.
Benchmarks show Zeroboot's memory usage per sandbox is approximately 265KB, significantly lower than comparable solutions.
SDKs are provided for Python and TypeScript to facilitate integration and usage.
The project is a working prototype but not yet production-hardened.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Zeroboot's architecture and performance benchmarks are consistent with the project's GitHub repository and technical details provided there. Firecracker is a known microVM technology, and KVM is a widely used virtualization technology in Linux, supporting the feasibility of the described approach. The stated performance metrics and memory usage align with the expected benefits of copy-on-write memory forking in VM environments. However, as the project is a prototype and not production-hardened, the benchmarks should be independently verified in real-world scenarios.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential security implications or limitations of using CoW memory forking in VM sandboxes.
There is no mention of how Zeroboot handles persistent storage or networking within the sandboxes.
The scalability and stability of the system under heavy concurrent workloads beyond the benchmarked 1000 forks are not detailed.
The prototype status indicates potential undiscovered bugs or performance issues in production environments.
Comparisons to other sandboxing or containerization technologies beyond basic latency and memory metrics are not provided.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the technical aspects and challenges of sub-millisecond VM sandboxes using copy-on-write memory forking. Commenters highlight security concerns like entropy duplication, practical deployment issues such as networking setup and cross-node cloning, and potential use cases and limitations.

comments.author: cperciva
comments.text:
Don't forget about entropy! You've just created two identical copies of all of your random number generators, which could be very very bad for security.

The firecracker team wrote a very good paper about addressing this when they added snapshot support.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out a critical security risk related to entropy duplication in VM snapshots, referencing a known paper, making it highly accurate and relevant.

comments.author: diptanu
comments.text: The tricky part of doing this in production is cloning sandboxes across nodes. You would have to snapshot the resident memory, file system (or a CoW layer on top of the rootfs), move the data across nodes, etc.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights practical challenges in production environments, emphasizing the complexity of distributed sandbox cloning.

comments.author: crawshaw
comments.text:
Nice to see this work! I experimented with this for exe.dev before we launched. The VM itself worked really well, but there was a lot of setup to get the networking functioning. And in the end, our target are use cases that don't mind a ~1-second startup time, which meant doing a clean systemd start each time was easier.

That said, I have seen several use cases where people want a VM for something minimal, like a python interpreter, and this is absolutely the sort of approach they should be using. Lot of promise here, excited to see how far you can push it!
comments.analysis: Provides practical experience and context about the trade-offs between fast startup VMs and simpler but slower alternatives, adding valuable real-world perspective.

comments.author: vmg12
comments.text:
Does it only work with that specific version of firecracker and only with vms with 1 vcpu?

More than the sub ms startup time the 258kb of ram per VM is huge.
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about compatibility and resource usage, though the claim about 258kb RAM per VM being huge depends on context and could use more detail.

comments.author: buckle8017
comments.text: This is how android processes work, but it's a security problem breaking some ASLR type things.
comments.analysis: Makes a relevant analogy to Android process behavior and notes security implications, though the comment is brief and less detailed.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text:
Your write-up made me think of:

https://codesandbox.io/blog/how-we-clone-a-running-vm-in-2-s...

Are there parallels?
comments.analysis: Poses a question linking to related work, encouraging further exploration but does not provide analysis itself.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text: Does this need passthrough or might we be able to leverage PVM with it on a passthrough-less cloud VM/VPS?
comments.analysis: Asks a technical question about hardware passthrough requirements, showing engagement but no definitive claim.

comments.author: handfuloflight
comments.text: Can you run this in another sandbox? Not sure why you'd want to... but can you?
comments.analysis: Poses a speculative question about sandbox nesting, showing curiosity but lacking technical detail.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.analysis: This comment is a meta-discussion about thread management and does not contribute to technical analysis.
